Phaistos Disk Final Engine

Phaistos Disk Game – ReadMe

Game Rules – Side A

Setup: Off-disk counts as 1. Players roll for unique rim staging compartments. Turn order follows the rim order of the assigned staging compartments.

Movement: Two dice are rolled. A move to a non-final track compartment is legal only when that landing compartment shares at least one available symbol with that player's staging compartment.

Occupation: After landing, the player claims one matching available symbol in that compartment.

Finish: Exact finish is required. A player finishes only by landing exactly on T19, the final rosette compartment.

T18: Landing on T18 puts that player out, but the game continues for the remaining active players.

Game Rules – Side B

Setup: Side B has 12 rim compartments, and the rim staging count begins directly with R01 as count 1. This differs from Side A, which has 11 rim compartments and counts the off-disk state as count 1. Because of this Side B revision, the highest initiation roll receives R01, while the remaining players roll for unique remaining rim staging compartments. Turn order again follows rim order.

Movement: The same symbol-matching rule is used as on Side A.

Finish: Overshoot wins. Reaching or passing the final winning compartment completes that player's run.

Multiple finishers: Each active player may finish independently; there is no single automatic winner by elimination.

Historical Note

The Phaistos Disk was discovered in 1908 by Luigi Pernier at the Minoan palace site of Phaistos in Crete and first published in 1909.

It is generally dated to the Bronze Age, commonly in the second millennium BCE. The Disk is a fired clay object stamped with repeated signs arranged in a spiral on both sides.

This game interpretation treats the object not primarily as a text, but as a procedural spiral race board: the rim serving as staging space, the inward spiral as track progression, and the repeated figures as playable symbol-matching positions.

Within this interpretation, differences between Side A and Side B may reflect iterative manufacture and rule revision rather than inscription alone, including the different staging counts on the rim and the different finish conditions.
